two questions about the twilight council.body:
1. The board says that you 'craft with assemblies'. So, the idea of having them craft with enemies crafting pieces has been abandoned? Why? Or am I mis-reading this?

2. It appears that any opponent can flip an assembly to closed for no cost except loyalists for the Council? What the heck? Is there a once-per turn limit? Card cost? Requirement that you have to be at the assembly. I don't understand.

Clarification would be great. Thanks, all!

Yes that was abandoned as it was impossible to balance

There is no frequency limit or cost for the player activating Entreat. The hidden cost is that using it wantonly will supercharge the Bats' recruiting

From https://rules.ledergames.com/?product=root
16.4.1 Craft. You may activate enclaves to craft cards (16.2.1.II).

That would be Negotiations.

16.2.5 Negotiations. Once during their turn, an enemy with faction pieces in a clearing with a Militant enclave may flip it to Peaceful. If they don't rule the enclave but another player does, the flipping player must give the ruler a card in order to flip the enclave.

From rules.ledergames.com
16.4.1 Craft. You may activate enclaves to craft cards (16.2.1.II).

That would be Negotiations.

16.2.5 Negotiations. Once during their turn, an enemy with faction pieces in a clearing with a Militant enclave may flip it to Peaceful. If they don't rule the enclave but another player does, the flipping player must give the ruler a card in order to flip the enclave.
OP was asking about the Bats, and you are referencing the Frogs
Oh thanks, that'll teach me to reply while not having woken up yet.

The answer is, there's no mistakes that's precisely how they work.

Play a couple of games and you should find they're well balanced (even a little strong imo)

Yes that was abandoned as it was impossible to balance

This disappoints me. How was it impossible to balance? It seems like it would encourage strategic placement of assemblies. Of course, they would occasionally struggle to craft but that seems like a reasonably thematic cost of playing without helpful allies. What is the problem? Where did they release this?

The answer to question 2 is very interesting. I wonder how the meta will turn out for that.

They already score from enemy cardboard, so strategic assemblies are still required.
The crafting thing was an issue because different matchups have vastly different crafting pieces. In a WA game it'd be fine, but against Cats, Vagabond and Keepers in Iron there may only ever be 3 crafting pieces on the board in the whole game.
Whereas the amount of cardboard is consistent across most matchups, in my afformentioned example, cats and badgers put down plenty of cardboard, it's just only a small percentage are crafting pieces.

Basically crafting pieces are ridiculously variable whereas cardboard (while still being variable) is far less so
Thanks for the clarification. I still would have liked the mechanic to show up somewhere. Maybe they choose which to use each turn? Or they use both?

Twilight council question.body:
Ok so the Twilight council banish action reads:

"Battle. Your hits force enemy warriors to move instead, ignoring rule. Ignore any rolled his you take"

So if I have partisans or any other means to deal extra hits, do those hits also make enemies move, or to they hit them normally? 

Also can you use that action to hit enemy cardboard? If so, do you hit them normally or do extra rules apply?

Thanks in advance

1) Extra Hits you deal will also make enemy warriors move

2) Banish does not deal hits to enemy buildings and tokens. If you move all enemy warriors and have hits left over, those left over hits are simply unused

Ok ty

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?

Ok ty

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?
No, Banish moves all hit warriors to a single destination

Ok ty

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?
No, Banish moves all hit warriors to a single destination
Excelent tysm

Ok ty

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?
No, Banish moves all hit warriors to a single destination
Even though its a much simpler system than it once was, it's still a little complex and very unique. Banish is so weird that I wish all of this was on the player board instead of just in the law. This is an instance where more would be more. A lot of people I've play Root with only check the Law for edge cases and trust the boards to (succinctly) cover what they need to know. Or they play most factions first in the app and then have no true understanding of how the minutiae of factions work. For some factions, the board is enough, mostly because the faction is very simple or comes from earlier days of Root when you could just take most instructions at face value. For others, the board is inadequate - someone needs to know the Law or know when to use the Law. After all, you don't know what you don't know. I find this to be especially true for Lord of Hundreds and all three Homeland factions. These are also factions with the most dense text, though, and I wish that it had been broken up a bit more. I can picture the Woodland Alliance board in my head. The same for the Keepers in Iron. There's a shape to their turn structure that helps you get through it. The Knaves have that a bit, but it's not quite as visually supported as the Vagabond's board. Even just having the buildings on the board, such as with the Lizard Cult, is a huge help for giving the rest of the turn a form you can live in comfortably. It would seem that I'm arguing for less text, but I am really arguing for graphic design that allows for more text because the user can situate different text in diverse contexts. Every time I see white space on a player board filled with a big animal picture opposite a wall of instructions, I just know I'm going to have to put extra effort into walking someone through their turn (or working to internalize that turn if I'm playing as that faction). Again, not a dig on the factions. Just a phenomenon that I wish was better addressed given how complex these factions are.
What is lacking from the LoH board? What rules do you think should be there but aren't?

What is lacking from the LoH board? What rules do you think should be there but aren't?
Precisely the fact that you cannot hit cardboard with banish.

I know the Law of root has it, but come on this should be at least in the manual

What is lacking from the LoH board? What rules do you think should be there but aren't?
Precisely the fact that you cannot hit cardboard with banish.

I know the Law of root has it, but come on this should be at least in the manual 
For what it's worth, the Banish text OP posted is not the final board text. The final text for Banish says "Battle. Ignored rolled hits you take. You only hit warriors, forcing them to move as you want, ignoring rule."

Knaves of the deep wood question.body: Ok so I know they haven't come out yet, but we're play testing them. So anyway, the Knaves board says that they cam move in and out of forests, but can they move from adjacent forest to another forest? Law of root doesn't really address this
Yes, they may move through forests - this has been confirmed on the Root discord.
Oh ty, what is this Root discord? Is like an official Leder games discord or something?
Yes it is officially sanctioned by Leder games and is called the Woodland Warriors.

This counts as moving into a forest.

Law of Root has rules for forest adjacency, and move into a forest abilities allow you to move into any adjacent forest, no restrictions based on where you're moving from. So forest to forest movement is well covered.

Vagabond can also slip from one forest to another.

The Vagabond cannot move from one forest to another.

9.5.1. If in a forest, you may only move into an adjacent clearing.

For clarification, the Vagabond cannot move to an adjacent forest in daylight, but they can still use the slip action to move to an adjacent forest during birdsong.

9.4.2 Slip. You may move into an adjacent clearing or forest without exhausting any Boot, even for moving into a Hostile clearing (9.2.9.IIIb). This move ignores all effects that prevent movement out of a clearing (such as the Corvids' snare).

Question about moles and Hundreds.body:
Ok so I have two questions:

1.- As moles when you dig a tunnel, is it mandatory to have warriors in your burrow when building it? I believe you can dig and move 0, but what if you don't have warriors at all?

2.- Hundreds, when you raze a ruin and is it mandatory to take the item even if you don't want it?

Thanks in advance

1. Yes you can dig with no warriors and the tunnel will be placed but you have nothing to move. However interesting to note you may not dig and choose to move 0 warriors. If you have any number of warriors in the burrow you must move at least one (this is an update from a rule clarification about whether 0 is a number or not with Homeland, it's a whole thing)

2. Yes, it is mandatory to take the item. The only way to get rid of it is with the Lavish mood or if your item track is already full. The items in the ruins can block some pretty good moods so that's part of the gamble!
Just to justify the above, here's the current wording in the latest version of the law, it's very clear in the latest edition 🙂

Just to justify the above, here's the current wording in the latest version of the law, it's very clear in the latest edition 🙂 

Tysm, does the law of root mention anything about the hundreds item being mandatory to take?

if Root doesn't say "may" in an action then it's mandatory. (with several weird exceptions)

So here's the list of rules you should know to understand why you must take the item:

So whenever a mob razes an item must be taken from a ruin without any choice on the Hundred's part. You don't control the Raze action it just happens, same for removing enemy cardboard you couldn't stop it if you wanted to. And whenever the Hundreds gain an item it must go in their Hoard (with the exception of the choice you get when you craft but that's a different rule) so therefore if you have a mob at a ruin you might get stuck with an item you don't want.

Then if you know other Hundreds mechanics, you can remove items with Lavish, or by overfilling your hoard.
I think this is false. But that would just be my interpretation of the law that your not aloud to dig if you don't have warriors to move.

I think this is false. But that would just be my interpretation of the law that your not aloud to dig if you don't have warriors to move.

This is wrong.

Doesn't say anything about requiring a warrior therefore it doesn't. There's no interpretation where a warrior is required.

Then is a keyword in Root for compound actions, it means you must have finished the first step to do the second step, and you cannot do anything in between. But doesn't have any requirements for fulfilment of either step. If the word was "and" (used in replace) it would be more ambiguous but even "and" is usually used synonymously with "then", in the case of something like replace (which does require all parts of the action to be possible) a special rule is required. So it's "Then" as in, after doing the first step, do the second step

You must move one warrior, if you cannot, then you cannot. This is just how Root logic works, if you're instructed to move and don't have the warriors, you can't move.

I can't think of any way to read this which would require a warrior to place the tunnel.

The Riverfolk Company's funds.body: According to the faction board, when the company loses a trading post, it has to return half of its funds. What happens if all the warriors are in the consignment area? Do they count as funds?

Nope! Funds only count as funds when they're in the funds box.

You only have to worry about losing funds from a destroyed trade post if you're trying to score dividends, otherwise it poses no threat.
Funds are defined as "warriors in the Fund box", so no, warriors is the Committed box are do not count for Trade Disruption, as they are not in the Funds box.

Not quite the only threat: If you attack on your turn in a way that destroys a trading post (for example, fighting with two Riverfolk warriors against three warriors at a trading post, and then rolling 3-3), you lose half of your remaining funds as well.

This can become relevant relatively easily when you run into an ambush.

Not quite the only threat: If you attack on your turn in a way that destroys a trading post (for example, fighting with two Riverfolk warriors against three warriors at a trading post, and then rolling 3-3), you lose half of your remaining funds as well.

This can become relevant relatively easily when you run into an ambush.

Yes, as happened to me in a recent digital game :(

A question about the Keepers in Iron.body:
In the last game I played, I had a question about this faction, which has been played very little in my group.

The player who was playing with them had three relics unearthed in the same clearing and, according to him, that meant that if he was attacked, none of his warriors could die because he had one less hit for each relic carried by one of his badgers. Is that true?

According to the faction board, the first hit received is ignored if you have a relic and a guardian, but it doesn't say anything if you have more guardians and more relics.

Ignore the first hit they take.

Your friend is wrong. Just get him to check the wording on the faction board next time the ruling is already very clear. 

It sounds like you guys already read the board, so if he still insists the effect stacks you have to ask why he thinks that 😅 Root rules are very precise and should be followed exactly as written.
Thank you. I figured it couldn't be because that would give them a tremendous advantage.
Yeah I just got shivers thinking about that lol

Woodland Alliance \"officer\" moving wit.body: Can a Woodland Alliance "Officer" do a "Move" action if the Alliance doesn't rule either their current site or the site he's moving to?   I believe the answer to be No but the two times I've played it seemed very easy to bottle the Alliance by placing warriors in the sites surrounding them.   Thank you in advance.
Nope, barring crafted card effects the Alliance needs to rule one or both clearings. Popping a big pile of warriors on top of their bases is often a good way to slow them down.

Only the corvids and vagabond specifically call out the ability to move without rule in the origin or destination clearings. All other factions are limited to that movement rule. 

On that note, one of the primary deterrents of the WA once they have a base is to camp out with a ton of warriors on that clearing so they can't move out.

They are bound by the rule of.. well, Rule, when moving.  So they either need to rule to clearing they are moving FROM or moving TO.  Hemming the Alliance in is one key way to help keep them from spreading sympathy.  Of course, they can still do it by spending cards, but with more Sympathy on the board, it becomes more and more expensive to do that (as opposed to using a Warrior to do it without spending cards).

Thank you to all who responded.  It is much appreciated.  I pretty much knew that would be the response but thought I'd get confirmation after seeing the way the Alliance was pretty much stymied when warriors enter their base.

Vagabond black and white pawns.body:
As you may already know there are two separate character boards for vagabond, one of them requires black pawn, and another one requires white pawn. One of these boards contains quest cards as faction components, another one doesn't. Also I have all available vagabond cards with pawns. Each pawn corelate exactly to one vagabond card (by art)
So here is my question. 
- Are those character boards completely interchangeable?
- May I use any white color pawns correlated to a specific vagabond card, if my character board requires a black pawn?
- Can I pick any of the vagabond boards if there is only one vagabond in the game?
- Are there quests in the game if I choose to play black vagabond without white vagabond?

- Yes.
- Yes. The color of the pawns is irrelevant.
- Yes. Both Vagabonds are similar.
- No. Vagabonds share the Quest deck, and playing with any pawn is irrelevant for the purpose of playing (other than aesthetics).
Thank you for replying, but what then is the purpose of specifying black and white pawns if it doesn't matter?

Originally, there was only one Vagabond. The Riverfolk expansion (2018) allows to play with a second Vagabond (that is, two Vagabonds in the same session), and in that case you needed to differentiate them, hence a black meeple and a grey meeple; there was never a need to match the card with the meeple. When The Vagabond Pack (2020, two years later) was published, now you could match the Vagabond you chose with the particular meeple, but that's that, aesthetics.

Thank you very much.
I understand it now. I forgot that initially it was literally the same pawn in different colors, and then the vagabond pack came in.

You can use whichever board you want with whichever meeple you want. Matching the board with the meeple was never a thing. (Personally, I didn’t even know the boards could be differentiated in any way.)

Usually you’ll want to match the meeple with the card, since each meeple actually corresponds to one card, but even that is not necessary. As long as you play by your card, you can totally use any meeple you want to represent that card.  

(Unless you’re playing a 2-vagabond game, in which case it would probably be confusing for everyone if either Vagabond’s meeple didn’t match their card. But you probably shouldn’t be playing 2-vagabond games anyway.)

WA: From where does a ‘trained’ officer.body: Does the warrior have to come from the appropriate base ON THE BOARD? Or does the warrior just move from Supply to the Officer’s section on the player’s mat?
Pieces are placed from Supply unless otherwise specified. In the case of a Revolt, it is not specified and so comes from supply. (8.4.1.III for Revolt and G.1.19 for definition of Place)
Yep! In fact, if you make an Officer out of a Mouse, you can’t use it for anything else, and you only get it back if you loose a base - so plan them carefully! 10 mice get used up very quickly!

How to remove the Corvid's Raid token on.body:
I was playing Crows and flipped a raid token face up to get some much needed points.

Fool!

Suddenly I was without one of the handiest plot tokens and everyone was giving it a wide berth. 

Is there any way for it to be removed from the board other than for an enemy to attack it? 

Google told me I could attack it myself but the Law of Root states that battle is against enemy pieces, so I don't agree…
You can only attack enemy pieces, as you say, and you're not an enemy of yourself. And yes, I think the only way to remove it is that enemies should attack it.
Because other factions aren’t incentivised to attack and remove raid tokens, leverage that to help you score points by flipping other tokens instead.

Don't forget you can also use the Trick action to move that plot somewhere else. (Provided you have a different revealed plot on the board during your turn.) Perhaps enemies would be more incentivized to remove it if it was somewhere else?

But I agree with the others — a Raid plot usually does more for you when it's revealed and boosting your scoring for the other revealed plots than it does when it's removed.

In response to the OP, I am not sure it is the handiest token. I wouldn't differentiate but if forced to choose it would be the one that gives you more cards (exploit?).

But Paul hits the nail on the head.....if you have 2 Raid tokens that are flipped and your opponents will not attack, that means every subsequent flipped token is scoring at least 3 points........they'll attack it soon enough

What happens if the Birds turmoil a 4th.body: Hi, what happens if you're particularly unlucky/inept as the birds and go into turmoil a 4th time? Do you shuffle the Ruler cards and draw on at random or what?
You get your whole ruler deck back and can pick any one you want.
Indeed, this can be found in section 7.7.3.I of the rule book.
Even the one you just had ?
Not that it matters much at that point. 🤣

Vagabond Coalition and Damage Rules Clar.body:
I’ve just read the Root Quick Start Guide and I have two questions that I couldn’t find answers to, even after checking rules 9.2.8 and 9.2.9:

1) Can a faction that is in a coalition with the Vagabond become Hostile if one of its warriors is removed, or once a faction enters a coalition is it immune to becoming Hostile?
2) When the Vagabond takes damage but has no more damage he can mark (and therefore may “ignore damage”):
if he is Allied with a faction and is fighting with the help of that faction’s warriors, can he still ignore the damage even if there are allied warriors that would be removed if the damage were assigned?

1. I don't think this can happen. If the vagabond is in a coalition with someone, they can't attack them, and therefore can't remove warriors. But also I believe the relationship marker for the coalition partner is removed anyway.
2. No, I would think that damage would have to be applied.

I don’t think the relationship marker is removed. (If you are hostile before forming the coalition, there’s even a rule to move it to Indifferent (see 9.2.9.III.D).)

And I think in rare cases the Vagabond can remove warriors from a coalition partner. For example, when the Vagrant makes their coalition partner fight using Instigate. (This might be the only way?) In this case, the relationship would turn hostile again; there is no rule making an exception here.

I don’t think the relationship marker is removed. (If you are hostile before forming the coalition, there’s even a rule to move it to Indifferent (see 9.2.9.III.D).)

And I think in rare cases the Vagabond can remove warriors from a coalition partner. For example, when the Vagrant makes their coalition partner fight using Instigate. (This might be the only way?) In this case, the relationship would turn hostile again; there is no rule making an exception here.

You are correct!

In any case. Why would a VB in a coalition with someone actually want to go hostile?
Probably they wouldn’t want to. But maybe Instigate is used as a Hail Mary to stop someone else from winning.
Can I also clarify that you can only initiate battle against an enemy and since coalition is no longer enemy, you cannot attack your coalition faction?

If you are also Allied with your Coalition faction as well though, you can battle with someone else and choose to have your ally take hits. When they've taken more hits in the battle than you did, they become Hostile with you.

https://rules.ledergames.com/?product=root&locale=en-US&printing=p16#9.2.9.2.4-taking-hits-with-ally
9.2.9 II D Taking Hits with Ally. In a battle where you treat Allied warriors as your own, you can take hits by removing them. However, if you take more hits by removing Allied warriors than by damaging items during the same battle, that Allied faction becomes Hostile (9.2.9.III) at the end of the battle. This rule overrides the condition for becoming Hostile in 9.3.3.

Question about the Lost City landmark.body:
Picture the scene:

• Moles vs Crows vs Cats
• Mountain map is being used, along with the Lost City landmark.

I have two questions about the City…

1) Can the crows recruit into it ever? 

2) In our game the Moles attacked the Cats in the City clearing (4vs4). The Moles attacked player was using his brigadier to do two battles. 

The Cats played a Rabbit ambush to remove 2 Moles immediately.
The dice result was 2,0 removing two cats.

So far so good.

Then, before the second Mole attack (using the brigadier) the Cats player wanted to use their crafted Mice Partisan card to deal an extra hit.

Was that a possibility or not?

Thanks in advance 👍
Before Guerric turns up as I have been burnt on this before - the Lost City is all suits at all times. I hope that answers your questions.

Yes, they can always recruiit into the Lost City. The Mechanism used is that you check if the clearing matches the card. The Lost City is All Suits, so it matches.

The card says "In battle in mouse clearings" and the Lost City is All Suits, so that qualifies as a mouse clearing. Yes it was possible.

An extreme example with the Lost City is where the Woodland Alliance can reveal a yellow and a red card to revolt with an orange base.

How that works, is that the revealed cards are checked if they match the clearing, which they do, and the base needs to match the clearing, which it does.
It doesn't matter that the revealed cards don't match the base that's put down, because the fundamental aspect is that they match the clearing.

I've seen this explained before, and every time I understand it, but it has yet to feel intuitive to me. Even though it is. 

Preconceptions are really hard to shake down. A lifetime of using rabbit supporters in rabbit clearings to put down a rabbit base does not prepare you well for something like the Lost City. Incredible bit of design, honestly.

Well said, Fabio! I feel similarly 🤪 when it comes to the Lost City! In fact, I think I bulked and rejected this (correct) interpretation when I first came across it because I was thinking that the LC should become a color (which implies that it has to be consistently 1 color for the turn. But that is not how it works! Thank you, Paul for explaining it so clearly: the LC is all suits at once! And so can support any and all suite-related needs all at once - even if they are different colors!

Question regarding the False Orders card.body:
I read it to mean move any number of enemy warriors from ANY clearing to ANY OTHER clearing, regardless of adjacency. My thinking is it would explicitly say "adjacent clearing" otherwise.

Am I correct in this?

"Move" is defined through adjacency, so no, you cannot do that.

Edit: "When you move, you may take any number (more than zero) of your warriors and pawns from one clearing and move them to one adjacent clearing."
You must also follow any of the ordered faction's move requirements, including rule (must rule the start or destination clearing).
No, because False Orders explicitly says "ignoring rule".

No, because False Orders explicitly says "ignoring rule".
Good catch.  Didn't have the card in front of me.  I believe other restrictions / abilities apply though.

Can I move the moles from one clearing with a hole to another clearing with a hole?  Could I move them to the burrow?  
If a faction has warriors by the raft, can I send them in the raft?  Do they get a card?

It's 1 move action to move from the clearing with a hole token to the burrow. it's then another move action to move them from the burrow to another clearing with a hole token.

If you are allowed to move them, such as with False Orders, then yes you can send them in the raft. The raft can only be used once per turn so you would not be allowed to use the raft if it's been used to move others, at least that's how it is on the same turn.

Whether they get a card or you get a card, depends on what it is that allows you to move them.

See Paul's answers.  But also:

You can move the Riverfolk along rivers and have the Keepers carry items along with them.  You can also move hireling warriors controlled by an enemy player (but not both the player's warriors and hireling's warriors...they are considered two separate "enemies").  

Note that you cannot move the Vagabond or Protector, as they are pawns, not warriors.

If the warlord dies from the ambush, ben.body:
hi, 

If the warlord dies from the ambush, benefit from bitter cannot be triggered, i.e. no warriors are placed?

Correct

Bitter's timing window is specifically after the ambush step of battle and before the dice roll step of battle

Where is stated that Bitter's timing is after an ambush? When I read the rules, I don't conclude that from the texts (card and rule 14.7.1). Why rule 1.1.3 (Unclear Resolutions and Choices) cannot be applied?

Where is stated that Bitter's timing is after an ambush? When I read the rules, I don't conclude that from the texts (card and rule 14.7.1). Why rule 1.1.3 (Unclear Resolutions and Choices) cannot be applied?
"Before rolling" has been established to mean "the moment before rolling" since Arbiter released alongside the base game in 2018. As of Homeland this has been explicitly listed in the Law of Root as Step 2 of Battle
Thanks a lot! Yes, now that I re-read the Arbiter, it's clear from the rule, that says "immediately before you roll", but the Bitter rule does not have the word "immediately".

Vagabonds and Hostility and Infamy. Oh M.body:
Just took my first plunge into this wonderful game, playing the Vagabond against the Mechanical Marquise, and am now seeking either confirmation that my understanding of this is correct or a clear correction if it's not. 

Absolutely loving the game so far, by the way, and am looking forward to trying out all the other factions both by playing them myself and playing against them as bots. But I really do like the rather RPG-ish feel of the Vagabond. 

Anyway, back to my question. Once I have taken out a cat warrior one way or another and thus made them "hostile," whenever I remove any of their pieces (warriors, buildings, and/or tokens) in a battle I have initiated in my turn, do I get 2 points for each one -- 1 due to their "hostile" status, the other due to my "infamy"?

Is it really basically that simple? I wound up reading various threads discussing and debating this issue, and became increasingly confused and uncertain as I did so, as there seemed to be no clear and final consensus. Ultimately, this is how I played it in this first "learning game," and lost in a close one (30-29).

So... Is that correct, or not? And if not, please correct me. 

Many thanks for your help on this. And Happy New Year to all!

It’s just one point per piece of the hostile faction after you remove the first piece that sets them to hostile. So once you’re hostile you’ll get two points for each piece of cardboard, and one point for each piece of wood removed in battle on your turn.

From the law of root:
Infamy. Whenever you remove a piece of a Hostile faction in battle during your turn, score one victory point. (Add this to points scored for enemy buildings and tokens. Do not score a point for removing the warrior that made the faction Hostile.)

Important to note that striking enemy pieces with a crossbow will not grant infamy points since that’s done outside of battle.

Thank you so much for your response. Much appreciated.

So, just to make sure that I am now clear on this: whenever you remove a Hostile warrior during a battle on your turn, you score 1 point; whenever you remove a Hostile building or token, you score 2 points. 

Thus that "Infamy" effect adds on another point only for cardboard pieces (buildings and tokens), but not any wooden ones (warriors) where you still just get the one point.

Correct?

You have it backwards

By default removing warriors does not give points, and removing buildings and/or tokens gives 1 point. When the Vagabond is Hostile with a faction, they will additionally gain 1 point for removing pieces of the hostile faction during battles on the VB's turn (due to the Infamy rule). Which thus adds up to warriors = 1 point, buildings/tokens = 2 points.

And just to be extra clear, "Hostile" is merely a relationship status. The rule that grants points for removing Hostile warriors is the Infamy rule. The two rules are related to each other, but Hostility does not confer bonus points independently of Infamy, Infamy is the term for the bonus points that Hostility, in effect, confers

As Guerric said above; Only cardboard pieces (square building & round tokens) of other factions grant VP by default. Removing wood pieces (warriors (or pawns which can’t be removed so it doesn’t matter)) won’t grant any VP.

Under the default rules of Root:
- removing one piece of cardboard = 1 VP
- removing one piece of wood = 0 VP

Adding the infamy bonus the Vagabond gets for removing enemy pieces in Hostile status during battle on their turn:
- remove cardboard with infamy = 2 VP
- remove wood with infamy = 1 VP

Also, as stated above by Guerric; Hostile is a just one of the possible status’s that the Vagabond tracks among each other faction in the game & Infamy is just the keyword name they gave the trigger of gaining VP for removing a piece in Hostile status (in battle on your turn).

So... if the Vagabond initiates battle against a Hostile faction, he earns 1 point per warrior removed, and 2 points per building/token removed, right? 

Seems like "Infamy" is essentially a functional part of the "Hostile" status/condition, and seeing it that way kind of simplifies things nicely for me.

Thank you all for your help with this.

Yes, infamy is one of the main functional parts of being hostile.

The other main part is that you must exhaust extra boots to move into clearings with hostile warriors (with the exception of Slip). Also, once hostile, you cannot improve your status via aiding—that is, you may still aid, but it won’t improve the relationship.

May I ask one more question about crossbow striking/infamy points? It's most likely a dumb question but I just got Root and played only 1 game (and I am the one who should teach it ... ) so you will forgive me ... 😉

I take it is this way:

1) if the Vagabond is in a clearing with both warrior(s) and building(s) or other enemy cardboard piece(s) of the same faction the Vagabond can use the crossbow only to kill/remove a warrior of that faction and not a building/enemy cardboard piece (Law of Root 5th printing 9.5.6). That doesn't grant the Vagabond any (infamy) point since it's not done in battle even if it's against a factions towards which the Vagabond is already at hostile status. Nevertheless removing that warrior causes the status towards that faction to become hostile even if it wasn't hostile before (9.2.9 III). If this is the case (striking the warrior causes the status to become hostile) future kills of that faction's warriors done in battle will all get infamy points while in a battle vs a non hostile fation during which enemy warriors are removed the 1st removed warrior would not get infamy points (it would be the warrior who causes the relationship to become hostile).

2) if the Vagabond is in a clearing with only building(s) and/or enemy cardboard pieces and no warriors of another faction he can use the crossbow to strike/remove that building/enemy piece since there are no warriors to strike (9.5.6). That grants the Vagabond a single point per general rule (removing enemy pieces) and no infamy point since it's done outside battle even if that piece is a piece of an hostile faction. Striking that building/cardboard piece does NOT cause the Vagabond to become hostile to that faction since only removing warriors does (9.2.9 III). 

3) If the Vagabond is in a clearing with only buildings(s) and/or enemy cardboard pieces and no warriors of another faction and he attacks those building/pieces in battle (causing at least one hit even if he rolls  0 for the defenseless rule) he gets 2 points per piece removed if it's hostile towards that faction (1 per general rule + 1 infamy point) and 1 point per piece removed if he's not hostile. In this case (not hostile) he stays at the same relationship status since he's NOT removing warriors but other pieces.

Am I correct?

Ty for the answers 😊

Do you need to show the card before you.body:
Hi,

Let's say after 3 wrong guesses, you know for sure the final plot type,  do you still need to prove you have the card matching the suit in the hand to guess the final type?

Technically, you still can forget the final plot type and guess wrong, and still have to hand over the card to the crow player? or you can guess without having the card which i dont think its correct rule?

Hi,
Yes you need to be able to Expose, and for that the mandatory pre-requisite is to actually show a matching card to the Crow player.
In particular, in you have no card, you cannot Expose, and therefore must battle the plot if you absolutely need to remove it.

As you noticed, you could still purposely fake-guess for whatever reason.

Also important to note, the Corvid getting to see your card is still an explicit advantage for them.
For example if the only matching card you had was an ambush, you need to show them that to remove the plot. They get to know you have that ambush, and you get to remove the plot. That's the cost of the expose action even if you guess the plot correctly.
And remember, "showing a card" does not just mean flashing it to show the suit.  It means they get to see and inspect the entire card.

Advanced Setup with no Vagabonds or Hund.body: The instructions to set up the ruins during basic setup are a separate step from any faction's setup. In the advanced setup, though, I can see no instruction to set up ruins except in the Vagabond's and Hundred's faction setup. If using Advanced Setup and neither of these factions are playing, should the Ruins slots be empty, or should they be filled anyway?
Set the ruins up as normal. It’s the last sentence of step 1 in advanced setup.
You put the ruins tokens alone, without any other token under them.
Vagabond and Hundreds actually instruct you to set up Ruin items rather than the Ruins themselves

Vagabond damages all his undamaged sword.body:
Hi,

If Vagabond damages all his undamaged sword after being ambushed as attacker, will the battle stop?
You still battle, the vagabond is just treated as defenseless. (9.2.4)
but defenseless is state that granted to defender, in this case  Vagabond is the attacker,  so can he be considered defenseless with no undamaged sword?

The battle will continue, with the Vagabond having a maximum rolled hits value of 0 but still able to deal extra hits from Defenseless or Crafted Improvements or so on

The attacking Vagabond is not defenseless, that is a condition that only applies to defenders. The Vagabond would not take an extra hit from the defender from having no undamaged swords
This question was already asked before:

Vagabond coalition with someone in a dom.body: So, previous laws of Root explicitly stated that Vagabonds could not coalition with someone in a dominance victory condition already. The latest one however no longer says that, and instead just says player with fewer points, and not having points is fewer than anyone else. I imagine they intend it to be like older versions, but the change in wording makes it a little uncertain whereas before it was crystal clear. Do we have any official guidance on the intention of the change in the latest version of the laws? We're playing it as though it's not allowed per the intention in older law versions, but I always like knowing for certain when possible.
Not having a point marker at all is not like having fewer. There is a "0" spot on the point track, and THAT would count as having fewer.
No point market is "undefined", not 0.  It is neither fewer not more than anyone else.

Regardless of whether things are undefined or zero, the game rules clearly spell out that the Vagabond cannot form a coalition with someone that has declared dominance.

9.2.8 Dominance Cards and Coalitions. In games with four or more players, the Vagabond can activate a dominance card to form a coalition with the player with the fewest victory points other than himself, placing his score marker on the chosen player’s faction board. If there is a tie for fewest, he chooses one tied player. If the chosen player wins the game, the Vagabond also wins. (The Vagabond no longer scores victory points. He cannot form a coalition with a player who has already activated a dominance card.)

Emphasis at the end is mine, for clarity.

The latest version of the law does not in fact state that, as I mentioned in my original post. The change seemed weird since it moved from clear to ambiguity.

Dominance Cards and Coalitions.
The Vagabond cannot activate a dominance card for its normal victory condition (3.3.1). Instead, in games with four or more players, the Vagabond can activate a dominance card to form a coalition with another player, placing his score marker on that player's faction board. (The Vagabond no longer scores points.) That player must have fewer victory points than each other player except the Vagabond forming the coalition, and that player cannot be in a coalition. If there is a tie for fewest victory points, he chooses one tied player. If the coalitioned player wins the game, the Vagabond also wins. (3.3.1 - Activating.)

I actually agree that this re-wording muddies it a bit more than it was before. But I still believe it's pretty clear. I think it's hard to argue that having no point marker at all is like having fewer. Having fewer would require you to have an actual number of points that is smaller than someone else's.

It is not clear from the rules that the answer to the question "how many victory points do you have?" is not a number for a player who has played a dominance card.  The rules say the score marker is removed and that the player no longer scores points but does not say that you no longer have a number of victory points.

I find it hard to believe the rule would be changed in this manner without being explicit, so I assume the change was driven by space constraints.  A phrasing based on forming a coalition with the player furthest back on the victory point track would be preferable.

It is not clear from the rules that the answer to the question "how many victory points do you have?" is not a number for a player who has played a dominance card.  The rules say the score marker is removed and that the player no longer scores points but does not say that you no longer have a number of victory points.

I find it hard to believe the rule would be changed in this manner without being explicit, so I assume the change was driven by space constraints.  A phrasing based on forming a coalition with the player furthest back on the victory point track would be preferable.

The point marker is literally removed from the track. What number are you arguing is the number of points that they have? 

Bearing in mind that there is a 0 spot on the track and their marker is not on it

If it helps, the phrase "He cannot form a coalition with a player who has already activated a dominance card." was removed in the 8th printing (Maruder expansion) for a number of reasons:

* It was redundant.
* The "Fewest Points" Requirement: Under the current Law, a Vagabond must form a coalition with the player who has the fewest victory points.
*The Status of Dominance Players: Rule 3.3.1 requires a Dominance player to remove their score marker from the track.
*The Interaction: Because a Dominance player has no marker on the track, they technically do not have a "victory point total" to compare. Official rulings (such as the Root FAQ and developer clarifications) have clarified that if a player's marker is not on the track, the Vagabond cannot mathematically satisfy the requirement of picking the player with the "fewest points."

By removing the sentence, the designers cleaned up the Law to rely on the core mechanic (the score track) rather than a redundant exception.

The Official Root FAQ explicitly addresses your exact scenario to clear up the confusion caused by the removal of that text:
https://boardgamegeek.com/filepage/176322/official-root-rules-faq

Q: Can a Vagabond form a coalition with a player who has activated a dominance card? Can a player activate a dominance card after a Vagabond formed a coalition with them?
A: A Vagabond cannot form a coalition with a dominance player, since they have no victory point marker on the track. However, a player can activate a dominance card after a Vagabond forms a coalition with them!

I'm not arguing that they have a specific number, but there are arguments to be made that the number is either what it was before their marker was removed as well as zero.  The rulebook doesn’t say.  Removing the point marker does not necessarily mean anything particular about how many victory points the player now has.

The FAQ seems to resolve the issue, but it's unfortunate that the principle is not found in the rulebook itself.

Removing their victory point marker from the victory points track means that they no longer qualify for anything relating to victory points.

It used to be found explicitly in the rulebook, but during an update it was considered better to remove it so that it was only the victory marker track responsible.
I understand it was Joshua Yearsley’s rule design philosophy to streamline the Law in the 8th Printing. His portfolio and various interviews explain the shift toward a "technical" rulebook that removes redundant "reminder text" in favor of core mechanics.

I like a technical rulebook myself.  I'm trying to explain how, by my read at least, it doesn’t quite meet that goal.

That's what it's supposed to mean, but the rulebook does not establish this.  The game could be different.  For example, a different game could have the rule that such players have zero victory points but the marker is removed as a reminder that they no longer earn victory points.  A different game could have an identical rulebook but a different FAQ that establishes a different intended rule.

Neither the actual FAQ or my hypothetical one contradict the rulebook.

I like a technical rulebook myself.  I'm trying to explain how, by my read at least, it doesn’t quite meet that goal.

That's what it's supposed to mean, but the rulebook does not establish this.  The game could be different.  For example, a different game could have the rule that such players have zero victory points but the marker is removed as a reminder that they no longer earn victory points.  A different game could have an identical rulebook but a different FAQ that establishes a different intended rule.

Neither the actual FAQ or my hypothetical one contradict the rulebook.

I agree with all this. 

This is what the currently shared homeland rulebook says:

If I'd had to make a ruling before today, I'd probably has suggested that the person has no victory points, therefore has the fewest. 

If it happens on Sunday 4th (when I next have root games planned), I might tell folk that a 2019 FAQ said you can't do coalitions but I'll let the rest of the table vote. 

Tbh, I'll probably just remove the dominance cards (along with favours) from the deck.
The rule has not changed.  You cannot, and have never been able to, do coalitions with someone without a victory point marker on the track.

The rule has not changed.  You cannot, and have never been able to, do coalitions with someone without a victory point marker on the track.

Seriously. They know the intent, and they are in this thread, why would they be confused?

The rule has not changed.  You cannot, and have never been able to, do coalitions with someone without a victory point marker on the track.

Seriously. They know the intent, and they are in this thread, why would they be confused?
I get that the rulebook could be clearer in this regard, but to vote or remove cards when the answer is known?  The rulebook may be considered slightly ambiguous, but it isn't contradictory.

For my part, as a computer programmer, I am keenly aware of the difference between "null"/"undefined", and an actual value.  A variable with a null value is not comparable (with greater than or less than operators) to integers in most modern programming languages I'm familiar with; if you try to do so, you'll get an error.

I know this doesn't necessarily "prove" anything from a board game perspective, but simply that for me, at least, it seems clear that "remove the VP" means "undefined VP".
